abstract:
Phenolic compounds represent a major class of biomass burning emissions that contribute to secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation through aqueous-phase processing in the atmosphere. This study characterizes the chemical properties of aqueous SOA (aqSOA) derived from the photooxidation of phenol, guaiacol, and syringol by two key oxidants: the triplet excited state of an aromatic carbonyl ($^3$C$^*$) and the hydroxyl radical ($\cdot$OH). Using a suite of advanced analytical techniques—including aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS), nanospray desorption electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (nano-DESI MS), and ion chromatography (IC)—we identify a complex mixture of low-volatility products. These include functionalized monomers, small organic acid anions, and an extensive array of oligomers ranging up to hexamers.

The resulting phenolic aqSOA is highly oxygenated, with average oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratios of 0.85–1.23, making them chemically similar to ambient low-volatility oxygenated organic aerosol (LV-OOA). While the molecular products are broadly similar across both oxidant systems, $^3$C$^*$-mediated reactions proceed more rapidly and yield a higher proportion of oligomers and hydroxylated species compared to $\cdot$OH-mediated reactions at the same stage of precursor consumption. Volatility measurements via a thermodenuder reveal that the stability of the aqSOA is driven by both its degree of oxidation and its oligomeric content. Furthermore, the aqSOA demonstrates significant light absorption in the UV–visible range, suggesting that the aqueous transformation of phenols is a potent source of secondary "brown carbon." These findings highlight the importance of aqueous chemistry in transforming biomass burning emissions into highly oxidized, light-absorbing particulate matter.
